images.caption: Figure 1:Structure ofPolicyShiftBench.(a)Dataset statistics organized by risk category and scenarios, highlighting the distribution of different policies across the benchmark.(b)Scenario–category policy matrix. Each policy is defined by combining a risk category with a realistic moderation scenario. Augmentation policies are used for Boundary-Policy Adaptation.

header: 2PolicyShiftBench: A Benchmark for Policy-Adaptive Image Guardrailing

images.caption: Figure 2:Overview of the PolicyShiftGuard pipeline.(a)Data recipe: images are annotated by three VLMs at the attribute level, merged by majority vote, and labeled by deterministic policy rules under the active policy bundle. This separates visual perception from policy judgment and creates same-image, different-policy label flips.(b)Training recipe: Stage 1 uses randomized-policy SFT to teach the concise guardrail interface, while Stage 2 performs boundary-pair policy adaptation on matched pass/block policy pairs for the same image and risk category.

Figure 3:Cost-performance tradeoff.We plot the main table Avg. F1 against decision speed, computed as1000/Time​(ms)1000/\mathrm{Time(ms)}. Moving upward means stronger average performance, while moving right means faster inference.

Figure 3:Cost-performance tradeoff.We plot the main table Avg. F1 against decision speed, computed as1000/Time​(ms)1000/\mathrm{Time(ms)}. Moving upward means stronger average performance, while moving right means faster inference.
Figure 4:Models’ performance across various risk categories.The heatmaps compare model performance across policy categories. The left panel reports F1 and the right panel reports accuracy.

Figure 5:Ablation over loss weights.We study the robustness of Boundary-Pair-SFT by varying three loss weights,λw\lambda_{w},λp\lambda_{p}, andλl\lambda_{l}.
This ablation study is conducted onQwen2.5-VL-7B.

Appendix GQuality Control and Human Audit

images.caption: Figure 6:Qualitative policy-flip case 1.Nudity and sexual-content example. The same image is permitted under a more context-sensitive policy but blocked under a stricter family-safe policy.
Figure 7:Qualitative policy-flip case 2.Nudity and sexual-content example. The policy change turns a pass decision into a category-01 block decision.

Figure 8:Qualitative policy-flip case 3.Violence, hate, terrorism, and self-harm example. Baselines often over-trigger a generic unsafe category or fail to follow the specific violence policy boundary.

Figure 9:Qualitative policy-flip case 4.Violence, hate, terrorism, and self-harm example. The policy pair tests whether the model can distinguish an allowed contextual case from a blocked category-02 case.

Figure 10:Qualitative policy-flip case 5.Regulated goods and substances example. The policy flip depends on whether the active rule allows static or educational presentation versus blocking the regulated-good cue.

Figure 11:Qualitative policy-flip case 6.IP, copyright, and brand-safety example from the Shift Split. The same visual evidence is allowed under one policy and blocked under a held-out IP/brand policy.

Figure 12:Qualitative policy-flip case 7.IP, copyright, and brand-safety example from the Shift Split. Although the image contains other salient visual content, the evaluated flip isolates the displayed category-04 policy boundary.

Figure 13:Qualitative policy-flip case 8.Cultural and religious sensitivity example from the Shift Split. The case tests whether the model follows a culturally specific policy rather than a generic safe/unsafe prior.

Figure 14:Qualitative policy-flip case 9.Cultural and religious sensitivity example from the Shift Split. The same image remains acceptable under one cultural policy but is blocked under another policy with stricter taboo constraints.

Figure 15:Qualitative policy-flip case 10.Privacy and PII example. Baselines often confuse the case with generic nudity or fail to block under the privacy-specific policy, while the correct unsafe branch requires category 06.
